annotations.text: The constellation of her symptoms prompts evaluation for acute porphyria.
This patient had gastrointestinal and neurologic symptoms; therefore, acute intermittent porphyria must be considered.
Lead poisoning causes a range of nonspecific symptoms, depending on the blood lead level.
Lead poisoning has been thought to affect several enzymes of heme synthesis, with the primary site of inhibition being ALA dehydratase.
Lead poisoning could explain the patient’s chronic gastrointestinal and neurologic symptoms, the absence of focal examination findings, and the presence of anemia with basophilic stippling.

An elevation in the ALA level but not the PBG level, as was the case in this patient, suggests ALA dehydratase deficiency, which is associated with ALA dehydratase–deficiency porphyria (an extremely rare disease), lead poisoning, and hereditary tyrosinemia.
I suspect that this patient had lead poisoning from ingestion of Ayurvedic medicines and that the diagnostic test was for a blood lead level.
Patients may be reluctant to disclose that they are taking these medicines or may not realize that it is relevant medical history.
The patient’s daughter visited from India frequently and had provided supplements to the patient in an effort to improve her health.
This patient’s blood lead level was markedly increased, at 104 μg per deciliter (reference value, <4.9), which confirmed a diagnosis of lead poisoning
The primary strategy for the management of lead poisoning is removal of the source of exposure.
